coredata.dc:title: Incorporation of biomass-derived carbon dots and hydrochar into electrospun polylactic acid membranes: A sustainable zero-waste approach to highly efficient methylene blue, malachite green and neutral red dye removal

Lee, Bong-Kee
coredata.dc:description: Recently, biomass-derived carbon dots (CDs) and hydrochar have gained widespread attention for environmental remediation. However, hydrochar is commonly regarded as carbon waste (CW) generated in the manufacture of CDs, and only a few reports have focused on the simultaneous application of CW and CDs. Herein, we propose a sustainable zero-waste approach for efficient dye removal by incorporating CDs and CW into electrospun membranes. CDs and CW were obtained via the one-step solvothermal carbonization of apple peels. The prepared CDs with an average size of 7.36 nm were first added to the electrospinning solution to obtain electrospun polylactic acid (PLA)/CDs fibers, followed by CW-integration via ultrasonication. The optimized PLA/CDs/CW composite exhibited high dye adsorption capacities of 141.99, 130.52, and 110.83 mg/g for neutral red, methylene blue, and malachite green, respectively. These dye adsorption capacities are significantly higher than those of the CD-loaded (70.15 mg/g for MB) or CW-loaded (52.34 mg/g for MB) composites, and are attributable to the rational loading of CDs and CW. Furthermore, the optimized composite exhibited remarkable chemical stability, stable reusability, and the ability to adsorb multiple dyes concurrently. This work provides new perspectives on the development of biomass-derived carbonaceous materials for environmental preservation and remediation.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of CDs and CW derived from apple peels and combined with electrospun PLA fibers to form the dye adsorbent.

Characterization of the CDs and CW. (a) TEM image of the CDs (inset shows the size distribution) (×195k magnification). (b) UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the CDs (inset shows photographs of deionized water and the CD solution under UV illumination). (c) SEM image of the CW (×200 magnification). (d) FTIR spectra of the CDs and CW, (e) XRD pattern of the CDs (black) and CW (red).

Characterization of the fiber composites. (a) TEM image of the electrospun PLA/CDs (white circles highlight representative CDs) (×38k magnification). (b) SEM image of the electrospun PLA/CDs (×1k magnification) (inset shows the magnified view, ×50k magnification). (c) Diameter distribution of the electrospun PLA/CDs. (d) SEM image of electrospun PLA/CDs/CW3 (×1k magnification). (e) Cross-sectional SEM view of electrospun PLA/CDs/CW3 (×200 magnification). (f) Tilted SEM view of electrospun PLA/CDs/CW3 (a portion of the representative PLA/CDs fibers and CW are highlighted using different colors) (×500 magnification). (g) TGA curves obtained under nitrogen atmosphere, (h) nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms measured in liquid nitrogen baths at 77 K, (i) BET surface area, and Barrett–Joyner–Halenda (BJH) pore volume of the samples.

Evaluation of the MB adsorption performance of the adsorbents. (a) Effect of contact time (20 mg of sample was added to 100 mL of dye solution at a concentration of 60 mg/L). (b) Pseudo-first-order model and (c) pseudo-second-order model fittings. (d) Effect of initial MB concentration (6 mg of the sample was added to 20 mL of dye solution at a concentration of 15–180 mg/L). (e) Langmuir and (f) Freundlich isotherm fittings.

Evaluation of the adsorption performance of the representative samples for MG and NR removal. Effect of contact time (20 mg of sample was added to 100 mL of dye solution at a concentration of 60 mg/L) on (a) the PLA/CDs and (b) PLA/CDs/CW3. Effect of dye concentration (6 mg of the sample was added to 20 mL of dye solution at a concentration of 15–180 mg/L) on (c) the PLA/CDs and (d) PLA/CDs/CW3.

(a) Nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherm of the CW measured in liquid nitrogen baths at 77 K. (b) Comparison of the BET surface areas of the different samples. (c) Dye adsorption capacities of the different samples (58 mg of sample was added to 300 mL of dye solution at a concentration of 60 mg/L). (d) FTIR spectra and (e) C 1s and (f) O 1s XPS spectra of PLA/CDs/CW3 before and after dye adsorption.
Schematic illustration of the mechanism of the highly efficient dye removal by electrospun PLA/CDs/CW.

head.ce:title: Incorporation of biomass-derived carbon dots and hydrochar into electrospun polylactic acid membranes: A sustainable zero-waste approach to highly efficient methylene blue, malachite green and neutral red dye removal

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Mechanical Engineering, Chonnam National University, 77 Yongbong-ro, Buk-gu, Gwangju 61186, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Mechanical Engineering, Chonnam National University, 77 Yongbong-ro, Buk-gu, Gwangju 61186, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Recently, biomass-derived carbon dots (CDs) and hydrochar have gained widespread attention for environmental remediation. However, hydrochar is commonly regarded as carbon waste (CW) generated in the manufacture of CDs, and only a few reports have focused on the simultaneous application of CW and CDs. Herein, we propose a sustainable zero-waste approach for efficient dye removal by incorporating CDs and CW into electrospun membranes. CDs and CW were obtained via the one-step solvothermal carbonization of apple peels. The prepared CDs with an average size of 7.36 nm were first added to the electrospinning solution to obtain electrospun polylactic acid (PLA)/CDs fibers, followed by CW-integration via ultrasonication. The optimized PLA/CDs/CW composite exhibited high dye adsorption capacities of 141.99, 130.52, and 110.83 mg/g for neutral red, methylene blue, and malachite green, respectively. These dye adsorption capacities are significantly higher than those of the CD-loaded (70.15 mg/g for MB) or CW-loaded (52.34 mg/g for MB) composites, and are attributable to the rational loading of CDs and CW. Furthermore, the optimized composite exhibited remarkable chemical stability, stable reusability, and the ability to adsorb multiple dyes concurrently. This work provides new perspectives on the development of biomass-derived carbonaceous materials for environmental preservation and remediation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Over 700,000 tons of organic dyes are manufactured annually worldwide owing to the significant expansion of the manufacturing and textile industries [1]. More than 10 % of these dyes are estimated to be lost to effluents during production and utilization [2], posing severe risks to human health and the environment because of their toxicity [3–5]. Consequently, the removal of organic dyes from wastewater is imperative for environmental protection and remediation. Among the numerous dye removal strategies [6–10], adsorption is highly preferred because of its efficiency, selectivity, and cost-effectiveness [11–13]. Dye adsorption involves the diffusion of dye molecules onto the surface of the adsorbent, followed by adhesion through molecular interactions and diffusion into the interior of the adsorbent [14]. Therefore, an ideal adsorbent should possess a large surface area and numerous functional groups to ensure sufficient attachment and interaction with dye molecules [15–17]. Currently, several adsorbent types, including metal-based [18], polymer-based [19], biomass-based [20], and hybrid adsorbents [21], have been developed to fulfill these requirements. Among these, biomass-derived carbonaceous adsorbents are regarded as one of the most promising adsorbents because they are eco-friendly, inexpensive, and abundant [22].

Biomass is a renewable organic material derived from plants and animals [23]. Carbonaceous materials derived from biomass have vast sources, including waste streams and zero-waste renewable streams. Among these, waste biomass, such as plant residues, crop residues, waste food residues, and animal residues, is considered particularly versatile because it involves a waste-to-wealth process [24]. Carbonaceous materials derived from fruit peels have recently gained significant attention because they are common everyday food wastes and are produced in large quantities in the food industry [25–27]. To obtain carbonaceous materials derived from fruit peels, hydrothermal carbonization is strongly preferred because of its short time consumption, energy-saving ability, and high-quality carbon product availability [28]. Hydrothermal carbonization is a thermochemical biomass degradation process performed at elevated temperatures and pressures in an aqueous solution [29]. Generally, this process produces two entities from the fruit peels: the supernatant-containing carbon dots (CDs) and the solid residue known as hydrochar. CDs are a class of zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials with a sphere-like shape and dimensions less than 10 nm [30]. CDs can provide a sufficiently large number of surface adsorption sites because of their small size and large specific surface area [31]. Moreover, the surface of CDs contains abundant functional groups—carboxylic, hydroxyl, and amino groups—that can undergo effective electrostatic interactions with dye molecules [32]. Owing to these outstanding characteristics, CDs have been widely used as adsorbents in environmental remediation applications in recent years [31–33]. Additionally, hydrochar is a carbon-rich solid that has a porous structure and a strong aromatic nature. Its surface possesses a high number of hydroxyl, carboxylic, and carbonyl functional groups, which are beneficial for the adsorption of pollutants [34,35].

Although biomass-derived CDs and hydrochar have been shown to be effective in water treatment, their simultaneous use is rarely considered. Aggarwal et al. reported the use of CDs from waste apple peels for dye photocatalysis, but did not mention the hydrochar obtained during CD preparation [36]. Tyagi et al. used waste lemon peel to prepare CDs for sensing and photocatalysis, but the hydrochar obtained with the CDs was not addressed [25]. Despite the excellent performance of these waste biomass-derived CDs, the overlooked utilization of the hydrochar resulted in wasted resources and additional waste generation. It is readily apparent that the hydrochar resulting from the carbonization process is often considered a carbon waste (CW) byproduct and is rarely used alongside CDs in adsorption applications. This can be attributed to the inferior surface area and porosity of CW, which usually requires additional activation processes to achieve the desired properties [37,38]. Nevertheless, hydrochar is effective in adsorbing pollutants owing to the abundant functional groups on its surface [34,35]. Therefore, we anticipate that the combined use of CDs and CW will not only optimize resource efficiency and minimize waste generation, but also synergistically improve the adsorption capacity, surpassing the performance of either CDs or CW used independently.

Although leveraging both CDs and CWs is highly rational and promising, their particulate nature presents several practical limitations. For example, van der Waals interactions can cause particles to aggregate, thereby reducing performance; additionally, extraction and separation can be difficult, making reuse challenging [39]. Thus, the immobilization of particulate adsorbents on suitable substrates has been proposed to overcome their application barriers [40]. Among the various types of substrates, electrospun membranes are a promising option and have been well-developed in recent years. Owing to their exceptional specific surface area, porosity, selectivity, and flexibility, electrospun membranes enable the effective loading of particulate adsorbents and also create avenues for enhancing adsorption efficiency [41,42]. For example, CD-doped electrospun substrates can provide additional adsorption sites when used with specific polymers, further enhancing performance [43]. Furthermore, CD-functionalized magnetic electrospun substrates facilitate the reusability of the composite without causing a notable decrease in adsorption capacity [44].

Recently, all-biosorbents have gained adequate attention owing to their biocompatibility and biodegradability, which aid in preventing secondary contamination upon use [16,20]. CDs and CW are fully compatible with these sustainability criteria because of their nature. In electrospinning, biopolymers are preferred over synthetic polymers because they offer the most promising solution to sustainability [41,42]. Therefore, integrating CDs, CWs, and biopolymer-based electrospun substrates is an exceptionally competitive strategy for creating an all-bio-sustainable composite adsorbent.

Herein, the simultaneous combination of apple peel-derived CDs and CW with electrospun substrates as highly efficient, sustainable adsorbents for organic dye removal is presented for the first time. To obtain the all-bio-sustainable composite adsorbent, polylactic acid (PLA) was selected as the polymer material for electrospinning because of its biocompatibility and biodegradability [45]. The fabrication of the PLA/CDs/CW composite involved a sequential process. Electrospun PLA/CDs membranes were first prepared and then loaded with CW through ultrasonication. Moreover, the effect of the CW content in the coating solution on the adsorption capacity of the final composite was systematically investigated. The optimized PLA/CDs/CW adsorbent exhibited significantly improved adsorption capacities toward methylene blue (MB), neutral red (NR), and malachite green (MG), surpassing the performance of CD or CW loading alone. The underlying mechanism of the proposed strategy for the efficient removal of dye was thoroughly discussed.

PLA (3100HP, Mw = 140,000 g/mol [45]) was supplied by NatureWorks LLC. Dichloromethane (DCM; 99.5 %) was obtained from Daejung Chemical Co., Ltd. Ethanol (99.9 %) and acetone (99.7 %) were obtained from Samchun Chemical Co., Ltd. The MB solution (15 g/L) and MG solution (10 g/L) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. NR powder (100 %) was obtained from Duksan Pure Chemical Co., Ltd. Fresh apples were purchased from a local supermarket (Gwangju City, Republic of Korea). All the chemicals were used as received without further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the CDs and CW
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs and CW were synthesized using a one-step solvothermal carbonization process, and apple peels were used as a carbon source. Fig. 1a shows the detailed preparation process, which was performed using a previously reported method, albeit with minor modifications [28,29]. First, the fresh apples were thoroughly washed and peeled. Thereafter, the apple peels were dried in an oven at 60 °C for 12 h and then ground into powder form using a kitchen grinder. Subsequently, 2 g of the apple peel powder was dispersed in 50 mL of acetone and ultrasonicated for 20 min. The obtained solution was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave, heated at 200 °C for 12 h, and then allowed to cool naturally. It should be noted that the currently used reaction conditions were determined based on previous reports [28,29]. The obtained CD solution and solid CW were separated using a 0.22 μm filter. The separated CD solution was collected and further centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 15 min to remove the solid residue. The final CD solution was stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for subsequent experiments. The isolated CW was washed with acetone three times and then dried in an oven at 60 °C for 12 h. After drying, the CW chunks were ground into powder using a grinder for subsequent experiments.

Fig. 1b shows a schematic of the electrospinning device used in this work. PLA solutions with a polymer concentration of 8 wt% were prepared. First, PLA was added to the DCM solution. The mixture was sufficiently stirred, and an acetone-based CD solution was added in the same volume as DCM; thereafter, stirring was continued to obtain a homogeneous solution. To compare the effect of the presence of CDs on the fiber morphology, pure acetone without CDs was used as a reference. During electrospinning, the distance between the nozzle and the drum collector was fixed at 15 cm. A high applied voltage of 15 kV was applied to the tip of the nozzle, where the polymer solution was extruded at a speed of 1.2 mL/h. The electrospinning duration was controlled to 2 h. All the experiments were carried out at 20 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Loading of CW on the electrospun PLA/CDs fibers
ce:section.ce:para: A schematic demonstration of the process of loading CW onto the prepared electrospun PLA/CDs fibers is shown in Fig. 1b. Fiber (50 mg) was immersed into 10 mL of an ethanol solution containing CW. The amount of CW in the coating solution was controlled to 1, 2, and 3 wt%. Ultrasonication was performed for 1 h to sufficiently load CW. The obtained product was immersed into 10 mL of pure ethanol solution, ultrasonicated for 10 min, and then washed with ethanol three times to remove the loosely loaded CW. The final samples are denoted as PLA/CDs/CW1, PLA/CDs/CW2, and PLA/CDs/CW3, which correspond to the concentrations of CW in the coating solution, i.e., 1, 2, and 3 wt%, respectively. Regarding the reference electrospun PLA fibers obtained using DCM and pure acetone, a CW concentration of 1 wt% was used in the coating solution, and the final sample was labeled PLA/CW1.

The morphology of the samples was characterized using scanning electron microscopy (SEM; Gemini 500 + EDS, Oxford) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM; Tecnai G2 F20). The diameter of the fibers was analyzed from the respective SEM images using image visualization software (ImageJ; National Institutes of Health). About 100 individual fibers were randomly selected for each condition. The functional groups were characterized using Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR; Spectrum 400, PerkinElmer). The crystal structure was obtained using X-ray diffraction (XRD; PANalytical X-ray diffractometer). The specific surface area of the sample was evaluated using nitrogen adsorption–desorption analysis (Belsorp mini analyzer). The thermal degradation behavior of the samples was examined in an inert nitrogen atmosphere using thermogravimetric analysis (TGA; TGA2, Mettler Toledo). Ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) spectroscopic analysis was performed using a spectrophotometer (UV-1900i, Shimadzu). The absorbance of different the dyes—MB, NR, and MG—were confirmed at wavelengths of 664, 530, and 617 nm, respectively.

Stock solutions containing various dyes at a concentration of 1000 mg/L were prepared before the adsorption experiments. In subsequent experiments, the required dye concentrations were obtained by diluting the corresponding stock solutions. All the experiments were conducted at 20 °C.

To evaluate the adsorption kinetics, 20 mg of the adsorbents was immersed into 100 mL of a 60 mg/L dye solution. The change in the dye solution concentration was examined at specific time intervals. To explore the adsorption isotherms, 6 mg of the adsorbents was immersed in 20 mL of dye solution for 6 h, and the concentration of the dye solution was set between 15 and 180 mg/L.

To evaluate the reusability, 10 mg of the adsorbents was immersed in 20 mL of a 60 mg/L dye solution for 6 h. After the experiment, the adsorbents were collected via centrifugation and immersed in ethanol for 6 h to desorb the dye. Subsequently, the adsorbents were collected through centrifugation and dried in an oven at 40 °C for 12 h. The final obtained adsorbents were employed for reuse experiments, and five cycles of adsorption–desorption experiments were performed. The initial dye adsorption capacity was considered as a standard, and the cycling adsorption efficiency was evaluated based on the standard adsorption capacity.

To investigate the effect of the dye solution pH on the adsorption capacity of the adsorbents, the dye solution was adjusted to a pH range of 3–10 by adding 0.1 mol/L NaOH or 0.1 mol/L HCl solutions dropwise. The adsorbents (10 mg) were immersed in 20 mL of dye solution with a concentration of 60 mg/L for 6 h.

To determine the adsorption behavior of the adsorbents in the presence of multiple dyes, 10 mL of MB, NR, and MG solutions with a concentration of 30 mg/L were mixed. Further, 20 mg of the adsorbents was added to 30 mL of the mixed solution. The dye concentration in the solution was examined at specific time intervals.

The adsorption capacity (q, mg/g) of the adsorbent was calculated as follows:

(mg/L) represent the dye concentration at the initial and equilibrium stages, respectively, V (L) is the volume of the dye solution, and W (g) is the mass of the adsorbent used in the experiment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the CDs and CW
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2a shows the TEM image of the CDs synthesized using the solvothermal method. The TEM analysis confirmed that the CDs exhibited a spherical morphology without noticeable aggregation. A size distribution analysis of the CDs revealed a narrow diameter distribution of 5–10 nm (inset in Fig. 2a). The HRTEM images of the prepared CDs are shown in Fig. S1a. The CDs exhibit a crystalline structure with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the C (100) diffraction planes of graphite. This confirms that the prepared CDs possess a graphite-like structure [31]. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the CDs is shown in Fig. 2b. The peak at 253 nm corresponds to the π–π* transitions of the aromatic CC bond from the carbon core of the CDs. The absorption band at 330 nm corresponds to the n–π* transition of the CO band, which is also responsible for the fluorescence emission from the CDs, as shown in the inset of Fig. 2b. These results are consistent with previous findings and indicate the successful synthesis of CDs [25]. Fig. 2c shows the SEM image of the prepared CW. It was discovered that the CW existed as irregular fragments and had a nonporous surface. The non-porous surface of the CW is attributable to the residual decomposition products on its surface, which cause pore blockage [46]. Fig. 2d shows the surface functional groups of the synthesized CDs and CW. For the CDs, the peak at 3417 cm−1 corresponds to the OH stretching vibrations, whereas the peaks at 2914 and 2846 cm−1 correspond to the CH bond stretching vibrations. Additionally, the peaks at 1651 and 1384 cm−1 correspond to the CO and CC stretching vibration bands, respectively [47,48]. For CW, a broad absorption band between 3400 and 3800 cm−1 can be observed owing to the OH stretching vibration. The absorption band between 2800 and 3000 cm−1 can be attributed to the aliphatic stretching vibration of the CH bond, which is more prominent in hydrochar. The peak at 1400 cm−1 may be caused by the presence of CH bending [49,50]. The presence of these functional groups in CDs and CW is expected to further enhance the dye adsorption capacity by providing sufficient sites for interaction with the dye molecules [31–33]. Fig. 2e shows the XRD patterns of the CDs and CW. The CDs and CW displayed an amorphous carbon phase with peaks positioned at 25° and 22°, respectively [49]. These morphological and structural characterizations demonstrate that CDs and CW with multifunctional groups were successfully synthesized.

The TEM images of the electrospun PLA/CDs were first examined to confirm the incorporation of CDs into the electrospun PLA fibers. Fig. 3a shows that there are numerous spherical black dots similar in size to the CDs (Fig. 2a). Additionally, the TEM image of the PLA/CDs differs significantly from that of pure PLA (Fig. S1b). In Fig. 3a, very pronounced spherical black dots can be observed owing to the enriched loading of CDs. The SEM images of the electrospun PLA/CDs are displayed in Fig. 3b. Fine fibers were obtained, facilitating stable adsorption performance. Additionally, highly porous structured fibers were obtained (inset of Fig. 3b). Such highly porous features further enhance the interaction between the dye molecules and the adsorbent, thereby enhancing the adsorption performance [12,51]. Moreover, fibers with a narrow diameter distribution and a diameter averaging about 300 nm can be identified in Fig. 3c. CDs have been reported to exhibit excellent electrical conductivity [30]. Generally, the electrical conductivity of the electrospinning solution has a direct effect on the product morphology [52]. Therefore, it can be expected that the presence of CDs will affect the morphology of the electrospun fibers. In this regard, pure electrospun PLA was fabricated for comparison. As shown in Fig. S2a, when CDs were absent in the electrospinning solution, remarkable beaded fibers were obtained. Additionally, the electrospun product exhibited a large uneven distribution due to the presence of numerous irregular beads. The formation of these beads is attributable to the utilization of the highly volatile solvent system, DCM/acetone. The rapid volatilization of the solvent led to insufficient stretching of the polymer jet by electrostatic forces, causing a bead-like structure; conversely, the addition of CDs increased the electrical conductivity of the electrospinning solution. The increased stretching of the polymer jet by electrostatic forces causes the formation of beadless fibers [52]. These results demonstrate the successful incorporation of CDs into the electrospun fibers.

Fig. S3 shows the SEM images of PLA/CDs/CW1 and PLA/CDs/CW2. It was discovered that PLA/CDs/CW2 had a higher CW content than PLA/CDs/CW1 owing to the increased CW content in its coating solution. When the CW content was further increased (Fig. 3d), no significant increase in the amount of CW was observed; this is attributable to the loading saturation. Fig. 3e shows the cross-sectional SEM view of electrospun PLA/CDs/CW3. The entire electrospun PLA/CDs membrane was massively embedded and saturated by the CW. Owing to its efficient CW loading, the PLA/CDs/CW3 composite demonstrated sparser stacking than the PLA/CDs (Fig. S4). It has been reported that such loosely stacked membranes are conducive to the penetration of dye molecules, which enhances adsorption capacity [53]. Fig. 3f shows the tilted SEM view of PLA/CDs/CW3. Almost all the CWs were observed to be stably captured in the membrane rather than on the surface of the membrane. Such stable capture loading is expected to facilitate the long-term stable use of the adsorbent.

The thermal stability of the composite was evaluated using TGA. As shown in Fig. 3g, the pure PLA sample demonstrated a one-step degradation accompanied by a weight loss close to 100 %. This is attributed to the loss of ester groups due to unzipping depolymerization [54]. Upon incorporation of the CDs, two major release steps were identified, and mass residues of the composite were observed. After CW loading, the composite exhibited a complex thermal degradation process. This is due to the hydrothermal carbonization reaction in hydrochar forming volatiles that are converted to other thermally stable components [55]. Furthermore, a pronounced mass residue was obtained at a heating temperature of 800 °C owing to the large carbon content in the CW. These results further demonstrate the successful incorporation of CDs and CW into the composite and are consistent with the morphological observations. The effect of the CDs and CW on the properties of the composite was further analyzed using the Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) method. According to the results presented in Fig. 3h and i, all the samples exhibited mesoporous structures [56]. Additionally, the incorporation of CDs increased the surface area and pore volume of the composite when compared to using only pure PLA fibers; this was due to the high specific surface area of the CDs. Furthermore, CW loading further enhanced these characteristics of the composite. This implies that CW loading has no significant effect on the behavior of CDs and enhances the surface area and pore volume of the composite, which are significant for improving the adsorption performance of the composite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the MB adsorption performance of the adsorbents
ce:section.ce:para: MB was comprehensively evaluated and chosen as the representative dye because it is commonly used in daily life and the textile industry [5]. The dye adsorption rate of an adsorbent is an important indicator of its adsorption performance. Thus, the adsorption kinetics of the adsorbents were first evaluated, and the results are displayed in Fig. 4a–c. All the adsorbents rapidly adsorbed the dye at the beginning of the adsorption process, as shown in Fig. 4a. As the adsorption experiments continued, their adsorption capacity became stable. This can be attributed to the abundant adsorption sites of the adsorbent at the initial stage of adsorption, which interacted with the dye molecules, causing rapid adsorption. As the adsorption progressed, dye molecules occupied the adsorption sites, slowing down the adsorption rate and eventually causing the adsorption capacity to reach equilibrium. Additionally, all the adsorbents attained adsorption equilibrium after about 2 h. This rapid adsorption of the dye further validates the versatility of biomass-derived carbonaceous materials for dye removal. The pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order kinetic models were used to further evaluate the kinetic adsorption behavior of the adsorbents [57]. Their fittings are shown in Fig. 4a–c, and the fitting parameters are summarized in Table S1. Based on the fitting degree of the models to the data in the plots and the fitting parameter R2, it was confirmed that the adsorption process for all the adsorbents followed the pseudo-second-order kinetic model more closely. This implies that the adsorption process is dominated by chemisorption [57], including hydrogen bonding, electrostatic interaction, and π–π interactions between the cationic groups of MB and the functional groups on the surface of the CDs and CW [56,58,59].

In addition to the adsorption rate of the adsorbent, the maximum adsorption capacity is another crucial criterion. Fig. 4d–f shows the evaluation of the maximum adsorption capacity of the adsorbents against MB. The adsorption capacity of all the adsorbents gradually increased and eventually equilibrated as the initial MB concentration increased. This can be explained by the fact that as the content of MB molecules in solution increases, the adsorption sites of the adsorbent gradually become occupied and are eventually completely covered, progressively increasing the adsorption capacity and causing eventual saturation. It was demonstrated that the incorporation of CDs enhanced the adsorption capacity of the PLA/CW1 and PLA/CDs composites more significantly than CW loading did. The structural characterization revealed that both CDs and CW have functional groups capable of interacting with MB molecules, and the high adsorption performance enhancement of CDs is attributable to their high specific surface area [24]. The adsorption capacity of PLA/CDs/CW1 was further enhanced. Based on the morphological and structural analysis results described above, such enhancement is attributable to two main aspects. First, the loading of the CW had no significant effect on the properties of the CDs, implying that the abundant functional groups on the surfaces of these two compounds acted synergistically to further enhance the adsorption performance of the composite adsorbent. Conversely, CW loading caused a change in the stacking behavior of the composite membranes. After ultrasonication, CWs with relatively large sizes were captured in the membranes (Fig. 3e and f), causing the membranes to become loosely stacked. This loose stacking allowed the dye molecules to sufficiently interact with the adsorption sites inside the membrane to further improve the adsorption performance. Fig. 4a also confirms that the adsorption capacity of the composite for MB was further enhanced as the CW content in the coating solution increased. This can be explained by the fact that increased CW loading provides additional adsorption sites and allows for a sparse stacking of the membrane, thereby increasing the adsorption performance of the composite. It is worth noting that although increased CW loading facilitates further adsorption capacity enhancement in the composite, it may be difficult to continue increasing the CW content after it saturates. The maximum adsorption capacity of the adsorbent was quantified using two commonly used models: the Langmuir and Freundlich models. The fitting of these models is illustrated in Fig. 4d–f, and the fitting parameters are summarized in Table S2. According to the fitting degree of the plots and the correlation coefficient R2, the adsorption process for all adsorbents was better described by the Langmuir model, which revealed homogeneous adsorption sites on the adsorbent and interactions between the dye molecules. According to the Langmuir model, the adsorption capacity of the PLA/CDs/CW3 adsorbent reached a maximum value of 130.5 mg/g, which is almost twice that of the PLA/CDs adsorbent (70.2 mg/g). This result demonstrates that the simultaneous utilization of biomass-derived CDs and CW for adsorption applications is highly effective.

In addition to the adsorption rate and capacity, the stability of the adsorbent in practical usage is also essential. In this regard, the reusability and the adsorption behavior of the adsorbents in different pH environments were further assessed. Fig. S5 shows the capacity of the PLA/CDs/CW3 adsorbent for MB adsorption in different pH environments. It was discovered that the adsorption capacity of the adsorbents gradually increased as the pH of the solution increased and stabilized as the pH approached neutralization. This result is consistent with previous findings and is attributable to static electricity [60,61]. In an acidic environment, hydrogen ions and cationic MB molecules compete with the adsorption sites on the surface of the adsorbent, resulting in a decrease in the adsorption capacity. In an alkaline environment, the strong electrostatic adsorption between the cationic MB molecules and the negatively charged adsorbent increases the adsorption capacity [60,61]. It is worth noting that in the practical application of dye removal, the effluent is close to neutral in most scenarios, implying that the prepared PLA/CDs/CW3 adsorbents can be stabilized and efficiently applied in a wide range of situations. The reusability of PLA/CDs/CW3 for MB adsorption is shown in Fig. S6a. It was discovered that the adsorption efficiency of the adsorbent for MB remained above 95 % after five cycles of use. In addition, the morphology of the samples after the repeated MB adsorption was investigated as shown in Fig. S6b. It can be observed that there was no significant change in the sample morphology compared to the unused samples (Fig. 3d). This confirms the excellent reusability of the current membranes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of adsorption performance against multiple dyes
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the above-described evaluation, PLA/CDs/CW3 was found to exhibit the best performance in this study; therefore, it was chosen for adsorption performance evaluation tests against MG and NR. Fig. 5a and b demonstrates the effect of contact time on the dye adsorption performance of the adsorbent. It was found that the adsorption rates of PLA/CDs and PLA/CDs/CW3 for MG and NR were able to reach equilibrium in a short period, similar to the case of MB. Additionally, PLA/CDs/CW3 exhibited faster adsorption rates than the PLA/CDs because of its numerous adsorption sites and more adequate interactions with dye molecules, as discussed above. The effect of the initial dye concentration on the dye adsorption capacity of the adsorbents is demonstrated in Fig. 5c and d. For both MG and NR, the PLA/CDs/CW3 adsorbent exhibited significantly higher adsorption capacity than the PLA/CDs; this is consistent with the adsorption behavior for MB. In addition, PLA/CDs/CW3 displayed the strongest adsorption capacity for NR, followed by MB and MG. This is attributable to the different interaction mechanisms and interaction strengths of the different dye molecules with the functional groups on the CD and CW surfaces [62,63]. Furthermore, the fitting results and parameters (Figs. S7 and 8; Tables S3–S6) confirm that both MG and NR are more consistent with the pseudo-second-order kinetic model and the Langmuir model. According to the Langmuir model, the maximum adsorption capacity of PLA/CDs/CW3 for MG and NR reached 110.8 and 142 mg/g, respectively. Table S7 summarizes the comparison of the adsorption capacity of the adsorbents fabricated in this study with those of previously reported biomass-based adsorbents. The PLA/CDs/CW3 adsorbent fabricated in this work was found to be superior to those fabricated in most reported studies owing to the simultaneous utilization of CDs and CW and their rational loading. These results further suggest that the simultaneous utilization of biomass-derived CDs and hydrochar for dye adsorption is a highly promising strategy.

In practical dye removal application scenarios, multiple dyes may be present in the effluent simultaneously. Therefore, adsorbents that can efficiently remove multiple dyes simultaneously would be highly desirable. In this regard, the adsorption behavior of PLA/CDs/CW3 for simultaneous NR, MB, and MG removal was further evaluated, and the intuitive visual observation is shown in Fig. S9. The color of the aqueous solution containing the three dyes changed from dark blue to almost transparent as the adsorption experiment proceeded, indicating the efficient removal of the three dyes. Additionally, the color of the mixture was nearly transparent at an adsorption time of 90 min, indicating that the prepared adsorbents can remove multiple dyes quickly and efficiently, which is significant for practical applications.

Based on the abovementioned findings, the exceptional dye removal performance of the composite adsorbent can be attributed to two factors: (1) the loosened stacking of the electrospun membranes induced by the rational loading of CW and (2) the adequate interaction of the CD and CW functional groups with the dye molecules. To verify the first factor, the nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms of the CW were examined, and the results are demonstrated in Fig. 6a. The CW was found to mainly possess mesoporous and macroporous structural characteristics, which is consistent with previous reports [64]. Additionally, the CW had a very limited BET surface area and BJH pore volume (inset in Fig. 6a), which is the primary reason CW has often been disregarded in previous studies. Fig. 6b shows a comparison of the BET surface area of the loosely stacked PLA/CDs/CW adsorbent with those of the densely stacked PLA/CDs and neat CW. The BET surface area of the optimized loosely stacked PLA/CDs/CW was found to be higher than the combined BET surface areas of the densely stacked PLA/CDs and plain CW. Theoretically, these values should be the same; nevertheless, the increased BET surface area of PLA/CDs/CW can be attributable to its loose stacking, which allows additional CDs with high specific surface areas to be effectively exposed. A high BET surface area implies increased exposure of adsorbent functional groups, which results in enhanced adsorption performance. This is consistent with the abovementioned findings. To visualize the difference in adsorption performance, the following experiments were performed: 50 mg of densely stacked PLA/CDs were coated by immersion in 10 mL of a 3 wt% CW coating solution (the detailed procedure is identical to that described in Section 2.4). The final mass of the dried PLA/CDs/CW obtained was ~58 mg, and this sample was designated as the PLA/CDs/CW adsorbent. Another 50 mg of densely stacked PLA/CDs and 8 mg of pure CW were combined and designated as the PLA/CDs + neat CW adsorbent. In this manner, the total amounts of CDs and CW in both adsorbents were controlled to be the same, and the difference in final adsorption performance was attributed to the effect of stacking behavior. Fig. 6c demonstrates the adsorption capacities of the two adsorbents for different dyes. The PLA/CDs/CW adsorbent was observed to display a relatively high adsorption capacity for all the dyes because of its high surface area, as confirmed in Fig. 6b.

To identify the interaction of the CD and CW functional groups with the dye molecules, FTIR and XPS were used to characterize the changes in the functional groups of the PLA/CDs/CW3 adsorbent before and after dye adsorption. The FTIR results presented in Fig. 6d show that the characteristic peaks of the adsorbent slightly changed after dye adsorption, indicating that there are interactions between the dye molecules and the functional groups on the surface of the adsorbent. For example, the adsorption bands of the –COO− group shifted from 1453 cm−1 to 1457, 1454, and 1448 cm−1, which correspond to MB, MG, and NR, respectively. This implies that electrostatic interactions occur between –COO− and cationic dyes [65]. Furthermore, the CO groups may interact with the aromatic rings of the dyes through n–π interactions, contributing to the observed peak shifts of the –COO− groups [66]. Additionally, after adsorption, the OH peak around 3334 cm−1 shifted to 3353, 3357, and 3343 cm−1, corresponding to MB, MG, and NR, respectively. The wavenumbers of CO shifted from 1043 cm−1 to 1047, 1040, and 1050 cm−1, which correspond to MB, MG, and NR, respectively. These changes were due to the generation of hydrogen bonds, where the abundant nitrogen-containing groups in the tested dyes served as hydrogen acceptors [67]. The high-resolution C 1 s and O 1 s XPS spectra of the adsorbents were also acquitted, and the results are shown in Fig. 6e and f. The C 1 s peak of the adsorbent is deconvoluted into four peaks, which are assigned to CC, CH, and CC at 283.30 eV; CN, CN, and C–O–C at 284.65 eV; O–C–O, CO, and CO at 286.00 eV; and COO− at 288.05 eV [68]. The slight variations in the binding energies of these peaks after adsorption (Fig. 6e) suggest that these groups participate in the adsorption process. This finding is corroborated by the FTIR results. The O 1 s spectrum of the adsorbent could be subdivided into three peaks at 530.65, 531.90, and 533.10 eV, which correspond to CO, OH, and CO, respectively [69]. The binding energies of these peaks also shifted upon dye adsorption, implying that these groups participate in the adsorption process. These results are also consistent with the FTIR spectral observations.

Fig. 7 shows a schematic representation of the proposed mechanism for dye removal. Overall, the rational loading of CDs and CW on the electrospun membrane ensures their effective exposure as adsorbents, facilitating contact with dye molecules. Furthermore, the abundant functional groups on the surfaces of the CDs and CW enable them to fully interact with the dye molecules through hydrogen bonds, electrostatic interactions, and π–π interactions. These combined effects contribute to the excellent dye removal performance.

In conclusion, we successfully fabricated an all-bio-sustainable composite adsorbent using biomass-derived carbonaceous materials and electrospun fibers for the efficient removal of organic dyes. The adsorbents loaded with both CDs and CW demonstrated much better adsorption performance than adsorbents loaded with only CDs (70.15 mg/g for MB) or CW (hydrochar) (52.34 mg/g for MB) and most reported biomass-based adsorbents (Table S7). Owing to the rational loading of CDs and CW, the optimized composite exhibited high dye adsorption capacities of 141.99, 130.52, and 110.83 mg/g for NR, MB, and MG, respectively. It was experimentally proven that the optimized composite demonstrated high chemical stability, reliable reusability (95.76 % of MB adsorption efficiency after 5 cycles), and efficient simultaneous adsorption of multiple dyes. The underlying mechanism of the excellent performance of the prepared adsorbents was also thoroughly discussed. All the results demonstrate that the simultaneous utilization of CDs and CW is a highly competitive strategy for dye adsorption applications. More importantly, the proposed strategy provides new insights into the use of existing and future biomass-derived carbonaceous materials for environmental protection and remediation.

Shichen Li: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Bong-Kee Lee: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition.
